mapping.a_element: proximity-induced superconducting state on the surface of a topological insulator (spinless p_x+ip_y superconductor)
mapping.b_element: low-energy effective Hamiltonian H_{eff} obtained via unitary transformation

mapping.a_element: coupling between s-wave superconductor and topological insulator surface states
mapping.b_element: perturbative decoupling of low-energy and high-energy subspaces via Schrieffer-Wolff transformation

mapping.a_element: emergence of Majorana bound states as low-energy excitations
mapping.b_element: ground state and low-energy excitations described by H_{eff}

brief_justification: While both papers involve deriving effective low-energy descriptions (superconducting state in A, H_{eff} in B), the mechanisms are fundamentally different: Paper A relies on a physical proximity effect and emergent topological superconductivity, whereas Paper B uses a formal unitary transformation (Schrieffer-Wolff) to perturbatively decouple subspaces. The core machinery—topological phase emergence vs. perturbative subspace projection—does not align.
